schools.summary: The forerunner as the sun's rays before sunrise; John 'the Voice' going before Christ 'the Word' (Gregory, Bede, Pseudo-Chrysostom, Remigius).
Chrysostom: John witnesses Christ's dignity by deeds; 'those days' is some thirty years after the infancy (Luke 3:1).
The synoptic narrative begins here with John's appearing (Mark 1:2; Luke 3:1; Acts 1:22), John about thirty years old.

outliers.note: The JFB note merged here concerns the wise men and does not bear on v.1.

schools.summary: 'Repent' (metanoeite) = change of mind/purpose, not mere sorrow — Robertson calls the English 'the worst translation in the NT.'
Wesley: the kingdom of heaven = the kingdom of God, a state to be enjoyed on earth, the disposition fit for heaven's glory.
Lightfoot/Calvin: a nation leavened with works-justification is called off to repentance; Matthew gives John's own words.

themes: change of mind not mere sorrow

schools.summary: The Evangelist's own citation of Isaiah 40:3 (though the Baptist used it of himself, John 1:23); the image of a crier preparing a king's way.
Calvin: the prophecy is not limited to John but certainly refers to him — the herald of Isaiah's promised restoration.
Wesley: 'make his paths straight' = remove every hindrance to his gracious appearance.
The austere camel's-hair dress and desert food mark penitence and sorrow for the human race (Jerome, Remigius, Hilary, Pseudo-Chrysostom).
The garb revives Elijah's (2 Kings 1:8; Mal 4:5; Zech 13:4); locusts a clean food (Lev 11:22).
Calvin: austerity is not John's chief excellency — his food simply suited his mountain dwelling.
Pseudo-Chrysostom/Chrysostom: his severe life preached louder than his voice; the Jews saw in him the long-failed grace of prophecy revived (Elias).
Remigius/Gloss: his baptism was preparatory like catechizing, not yet forgiving sins.
'All Judaea' = great multitudes from Jerusalem and the whole Jordan valley (Peraea, Samaria, Galilee, Gaulonitis).
'Baptized' = washed/cleansed (Jewish ablution, Heb 9:10); the imperfect shows crowd after crowd, confessing in close connection with the act.
Calvin: as God pledges himself in the sacrament, so we reply by confession; Lightfoot: the conflux owed to the expected Messiah (Daniel's weeks).

debates.question: Was John's baptism by immersion or sprinkling?

sides.position: doubtful for such numbers; possibly sprinkling/affusion

schools.summary: John as physician reads their hidden evil; 'vipers' names them from venomous imitation of their fathers (Pseudo-Chrysostom, Remigius); God's 'wrath' is not passion (Augustine).
The standard account of the Pharisees (Separatists, strict in tradition) and Sadducees (denying resurrection); the rival sects unite against John (cf. 16:1).
Calvin/Henry: John applies repentance to individuals, refusing to flatter the leaders, warning of wrath.

outliers.note: The JFB note merged here concerns the wise men and does not bear on v.7.

schools.summary: 'Fruit worthy of repentance' = the acts that result from a changed heart, demanded as proof before baptism (Ellicott corrects the ambiguous English).
Calvin: repentance attested by words is worthless unless proved by conduct.
Wesley: legal repentance (conviction of sin) here, distinct from evangelical (change of heart and life).
John strikes their pride in Abrahamic descent (cf. John 8:33-39); 'think not to say' a Talmudic idiom; descent avails nothing.
Jerome: 'these stones' = the Gentiles for their hardness — God can raise true children of Abraham from stones, by grace not descent.
Calvin/Wesley: presumption on descent lulls hypocrites asleep in their sins.

themes: we have Abraham to our father

schools.summary: The axe already 'lies at the root' (prophetic present); trees = nations/characters (Isa 5:1-7); Lightfoot reads the Roman judgment.
Clarke/Wesley: not a moment to spare — turn now or be ruined; the woodman ready to fell the fruitless tree.
Calvin: God is about to drive the unworthy from the Church as barren trees.
John's water cannot forgive sins (Gregory); Christ comes after him in five points (Remigius); 'unworthy to bear his shoes' = John the friend, Christ the Bridegroom (Jerome).
Water-baptism unto repentance vs the Coming One's double baptism 'in the Holy Spirit and fire.'
Wesley: the Spirit filling and the fire of love, visibly fulfilled at Pentecost (Acts 2:3).

themes: baptize with water vs Spirit and fire

schools.summary: A life-like threshing-floor image — fan, wheat, garner, chaff, fire — the fire being judgment at the Messiah's coming.
Calvin: having preached grace (v.11), John now preaches judgment to terrify the despisers who reject Christ's grace.
Clarke: the Romans are God's 'fan' as elsewhere his axe (v.10) and armies (22:7).
The sinless One baptized not to be cleansed but to cleanse the water and sanction John's baptism (Augustine, Ambrose, Jerome); baptized as man, not God (Hilary).
Chrysostom: the Lord with the servants, the Judge with the criminals — exaltation shining in humiliation.
Calvin: he received our baptism to assure believers they are ingrafted into his body and buried with him (Rom 6:4).
John 'tried to prevent' him (conative imperfect), recognizing him before the sign; he confesses his own need and unworthiness.
Calvin: John knew Christ as the Son of God, not merely a prophet; the Spirit's descent confirmed rather than first revealed it.

themes: I have need to be baptized of thee

schools.summary: 'Suffer it now' links the two ministries; 'all righteousness' = every righteous ordinance, sanctioned by Christ's example.
Wesley: it becomes both — Christ to receive, John to administer — that every part of the divine will be performed.
Christ's baptism dedicates the laver and opens heaven for the baptized; the heavens opened 'to the spiritual eye' (Jerome, Ambrose, Rabanus).
Uncertain whether the Spirit took a dove's form or descended dove-like; Luke has 'bodily form as a dove' (3:22); heavens opened while he prayed (3:21).
Calvin: a manifestation of heavenly glory, John seeing beyond the visible heavens.
The Trinity manifested — Son baptized, Spirit as dove, Father's voice; one nature, three Persons (Jerome, Ambrose, Pseudo-Augustine).
The Father's voice to the Son (repeated at the Transfiguration, Mt 17:5; 2 Pet 1:17) accredits Jesus at his Messianic entrance; a threefold testimony to his offices.
Wesley: a glorious manifestation of the ever-blessed Trinity — Father speaking, Son spoken to, Spirit descending.
